commands.repair_reason: - [P1] Manual review is needed because the remaining blockers include contributor-provided real behavior proof plus durable-memory/security-boundary decisions that automation cannot fully validate.; Needs attention: The diff introduces model-facing and operator-write memory file paths that still need target and conflict-safety hardening.; Review finding: [P1] Import the numeric reader before using audit tools — exte... (sha=2bf6d6d6a81d9fc831f35ef03286a040dba612b4)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a Memory Audit review queue across memory-core audit tools, managed cron scheduling, Gateway doctor methods, dashboard settings/review UI, docs, labeler, and i18n.

PR surface: Source +5695, Tests +1616, Docs +121, Config +9. Total +7441 across 75 files.

Reproducibility: not applicable. as a bug reproduction; this is a new feature PR. The merge blockers are source-reproducible from the PR head: `readNumberParam` is unresolved, targets are not constrained to collected writable surfaces, and missing hashes bypass conflict checks.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Manual review is needed because the remaining blockers include contributor-provided real behavior proof plus durable-memory/security-boundary decisions that automation cannot fully validate.

target.title: feat(memory): add Memory Audit review queue
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Adds Memory Audit as a human-reviewed memory quality pass for cleanup, correction, and promotion from daily/session evidence into durable targets.
- Exposes audit collection/staging tools, Gateway doctor methods, and a dashboard Audit tab with review and settings flows.
- Adds managed daily/weekly audit cron reconciliation while keeping Memory Audit separate from Dreaming promotion.

Why does this matter now?

- Dreaming is useful as an automated promotion loop, but in practice it can promote low-value or stale fragments into durable memory, and it does not put those writes through a human review queue.
- Memory Audit is the safer alternative for memory quality work: it uses an LLM to inspect writable memory targets plus daily memory and session-log evidence, stage add/edit/delete/move recommendations, and leave the final apply/reject decision to a human.
- The current PR targets `AGENTS.md`, `MEMORY.md`, `USER.md`, `TOOLS.md`, and `shared-memory.md`, while treating `memory/*.md` daily files and session logs as read-only evidence for promotion.

What is the intended outcome?

- Operators can enable Memory Audit, review staged recommendations, apply/reject items, and let managed cron jobs collect/stage future suggestions from daily memories and session logs into the right writable target.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Replacing or removing Dreaming.
- Mutating raw session logs or historical daily memory files during apply.
- Adding `IDENTITY.md` and `SOUL.md` as a targets in this PR.
- Live production rollout proof beyond local Gateway/config/test validation.

What does success look like?

- Memory Audit settings and review UI work through the dashboard.
- The owning memory plugin is enabled when Memory Audit is enabled.
- Managed audit cron jobs reconcile reliably after Gateway restart/startup.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Config/restart behavior for `plugins.entries.memory-core.config.memoryAudit`.
- Gateway doctor method scope and handler registration.
- Memory Audit apply/reject behavior and file-surface safety.
- Dashboard settings/review UX and cron reconciliation behavior.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

- None.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

- None.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- No explicit linked issue in this PR body.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Memory Audit adds a human-reviewed memory cleanup queue, audits daily memory and session-log evidence, stages promotion into `AGENTS.md`/`MEMORY.md`/`USER.md`/`TOOLS.md`/shared memory, and fixes enablement so enabling audit also enables the owning memory plugin and requests a safe Gateway restart for schedule reconciliation.
- Real environment tested: local source checkout in the `feature/memory-audit` worktree after rebasing on latest `upstream/main`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit-cron.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/memory-audit.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts`
  - `pnpm ui:i18n:sync`
  - `pnpm ui:i18n:check`
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit-cron.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.ts ui/src/i18n/locales/*.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.ts ui/src/ui/views/memory-audit.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/format-docs.mjs docs/concepts/memory-audit.md`
  - `git diff --check`
  - Scope check: `git diff --name-only upstream/main...HEAD | rg '^(src/tasks/|extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager)' || true`
- Evidence after fix: all targeted passing commands above passed locally; the scope check returned no task-flow or QMD mcporter files.
- Observed result after fix: Memory Audit tests cover `AGENTS.md`, daily memory, session-log evidence, read-only evidence safety, and promotion into writable targets; UI tests cover new kind labels; cron tests cover the updated audit prompt and startup retry reconciliation; gateway reload tests preserve the Memory Audit restart-required contract.
- What was not tested: full end-to-end dashboard interaction in a browser and a live Gateway restart that creates persistent audit cron jobs from a real operator click. `src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts` was attempted but could not import in this worktree because `src/gateway/protocol` is absent.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: local tests and source-level validation only; no screenshots or remote/live artifact attached.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): local diagnosis showed `memoryAudit.enabled=true` while `plugins.entries.memory-core.enabled=false`, with no Memory Audit cron jobs present.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit-cron.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/memory-audit.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts`
- `pnpm ui:i18n:sync`
- `pnpm ui:i18n:check`
- `git diff --check`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Memory Audit review queue, apply/reject behavior, `AGENTS.md` target handling, daily memory/session-log evidence collection, read-only source safety, Gateway doctor payload typing, dashboard controller/view coverage, managed cron reconciliation, i18n output, and config reload planning.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Enabling Memory Audit could leave `memory-core` disabled, so the dashboard looked enabled while plugin hooks/tools and managed cron jobs were unavailable.
- Audit cron reconciliation only ran at `gateway_start` and could miss cron service availability during startup ordering.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added and updated.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

What is the highest-risk area?

- New Gateway doctor methods/tools and managed cron scheduling that can inspect durable memory surfaces and stage recommendations.

How is that risk mitigated?

- Suggestions are staged for human review rather than applied automatically.
- Gateway method scopes and handler registration are covered by tests.
- Memory Audit config changes retain the restart-required contract and managed cron reconciliation has focused coverage.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Draft PR for maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- CI and any maintainer-requested live/dashboard proof.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- None yet.

<!-- agent-transcript:start -->
## Agent Transcript

<details>
<summary>Redacted codex session transcript</summary>

````text
source: [LOCAL_SESSION]
redaction: local paths, emails, phone-shaped strings, token-shaped strings, auth headers, auth query params
omitted: raw tool outputs, system/developer prompts, local paths, secrets, browser/session/auth details
stats: {"agent":"codex","entries":17,"user":8,"assistant":8,"toolCalls":398,"toolOutputsDropped":397,"web":0,"redactions":0,"omittedUnsafe":0,"rawEntries":65}

[user]
I have a local branch in its worktree: feature/memory-audit, and it is combined into my current branch feature/combined-flow-qmd-memory-audit-20260527
  I have this enabled in my openclaw dashboard, however, I think when I enabled it, it says it will restart gateway but it actually did not restart the gateway (and is restarting
  necesary?); Also, there are no cron jobs created related to memory audit as the feature intended, why?

[assistant]
I’ll first ground this in the actual branch/worktree state and the memory-audit implementation, then I’ll separate “current behavior” from “intended behavior.” I’m using the OpenClaw debugging workflow for this investigation.

The current checkout is on `feature/combined-flow-qmd-memory-audit-20260527`, ahead 13 and behind upstream/main by 270; the separate `feature/memory-audit` worktree is `/home/syu/openclaw-memory-audit`. I’m narrowing the search now because the first broad scan hit generated/bundled content.

The branch adds Memory Audit in 61 files, including the dashboard, `memory-core`, gateway config reload, and docs. The key clue so far: Memory Audit config is explicitly marked as a restart-required config prefix in the gateway reload planner, so I’m checking whether the dashboard’s “restart scheduled” message actually maps to a real service restart.

Two independent restart paths exist: `config.patch` can directly schedule SIGUSR1 only for `gateway.reload.mode=hot` or `off`, while the normal/hybrid path relies on the running gateway’s config watcher to detect the persisted config write and then request restart. I’m now checking why the cron reconciliation is gated on startup rather than on a hot plugin reload.

The live read-only checks show Memory Audit config is enabled with daily and weekly schedules, but `cron list` reports a warning that `plugins.entries.memory-core` is disabled while config is present, and there are 0 Memory Audit jobs among 19 cron jobs. That makes plugin activation the leading cause rather than cron itself.

There is also a startup race: Memory Audit only reconciles cron in a `gateway_start` hook, and that hook can run before the cron service is exposed. Dreaming already has retry/runtime reconciliation for this exact class of problem; Memory Audit currently does not.

I have the main shape now: this is not just “Gateway did not restart”; your local config appears to have Memory Audit enabled inside the plugin config while the owning `memory-core` plugin is disabled. I’m polling the live Gateway RPC state next so the plan distinguishes config-on-disk from what the running Gateway actually sees.

The live RPC checks match the disk config: Gateway reports cron itself is enabled with 19 jobs, but zero audit jobs, and the live config has `plugins.entries.memory-core.enabled=false` while `plugins.entries.memory-core.config.memoryAudit.enabled=true`. I’m grabbing exact source references now so the plan can pin this to implementation behavior rather than guesswork.

The source references line up with the symptom. The dashboard reads `memoryAudit.enabled` without checking whether the owning plugin entry is disabled, and Memory Audit’s cron reconciliation only runs from a `gateway_start` hook; the existing Dreaming scheduler already has retry logic for the same cron-startup race, but Memory Audit does not.

I’ll keep the plan on the lower-risk path you chose: preserve the restart-required contract, but make the UI honest about the disabled plugin state and make the post-restart cron reconciliation reliable. I’m checking the existing Gateway restart RPC surface so the plan can say exactly whether the dashboard should request a restart or only tell the operator to restart.

[user]
Implement the plan.

[assistant]
I’ll implement the approved plan now. I’m using the execution and TDD skills so the changes stay scoped and get behavior proof before closeout.

I reviewed the plan against the current branch. The main adjustment is that I’ll keep the restart-required contract, but I’ll use the existing `gateway.restart.request` RPC from the dashboard after saving so the message matches what actually happened.

I’m adding the red tests first: one for the exact disabled-plugin mismatch from your machine, and one for the dashboard requesting a safe restart after the settings patch.

The red UI tests fail for the expected reasons: the controller currently reports the nested audit flag as enabled even when `memory-core` is disabled, and it never calls the restart RPC. I’m making the minimal controller changes now.

The UI slice is green. I’m moving to Memory Audit cron and adding a failing test for the startup ordering issue: `gateway_start` fires before cron is available, then cron appears a few seconds later.

The first cron red test exposed a Vitest runner hang around the missing helper shape, so I’m moving to the implementation that test was meant to drive and will verify it with the completed helper export. The actual behavior target remains the same: startup sees no cron, retry resolves cron, managed audit job appears.

The memory test was hanging because the generic Vitest entrypoint was selecting the broad root config; the memory-extension config runs the file normally. I’m restoring the cron retry test and will verify it with `test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts`.

Both focused behavior suites are now implemented; the memory retry test passes under the memory-extension Vitest config. I’m formatting the touched files, then I’ll run the targeted tests again plus the existing reload-plan test.

Implemented the Memory Audit fix.

What changed:
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.ts` now treats Memory Audit as off if `memory-core` itself is disabled, even if nested `memoryAudit.enabled` is true.
- Saving enabled Memory Audit now writes `plugins.entries.<id>.enabled=true`.
- After save, the dashboard requests a safe Gateway restart via `gateway.restart.request` when that method is advertised.
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit-cron.ts` now retries cron reconciliation after `gateway_start` if cron is not available yet, and keeps a bounded runtime reconciliation loop with cleanup on `gateway_stop`.

Verification run:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit-cron.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check ...`
- `git diff --check`

All passed. I did not restart your local Gateway or mutate your local OpenClaw config.

[user]
so if I try to tuen on the memory audit, will it also turn on memory core if it is diabled

[assistant]
I’m checking the exact current controller lines so the answer matches the branch you have open, not just the earlier plan.

Yes. With the fix on this branch, turning on Memory Audit will also write `enabled: true` for the owning memory plugin entry.

Specifically:
- The dashboard now treats Memory Audit as off if `memory-core` is disabled, even if nested `memoryAudit.enabled` is true: `ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.ts:192`.
- When you toggle Memory Audit on and save, the config patch includes `plugins.entries.<memoryPluginId>.enabled = true`: `ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.ts:236`.
- It also saves `memoryAudit.enabled = true` and requests a Gateway restart afterward.

Turning Memory Audit off does not disable `memory-core`; it only disables the audit config.

[user]
and the fix is on both * feature/combined-flow-qmd-memory-audit-20260527 and
  feature/memory-audit branches?

[assistant]
I’ll verify both worktrees directly. Since the implementation was made in the current worktree, I need to check whether the separate `feature/memory-audit` worktree has the same file changes or not.

The current worktree is clean and contains the fix. The separate memory-audit worktree is detached right now, and my first search shows only the UI half there, not the cron retry half, so I’m checking the actual branch refs next.

No.

- `feature/combined-flow-qmd-memory-audit-20260527`: yes. It has commit `0ab394ea16 fix(memory-audit): cron retry reconciliation + UI safe-restart gating`.
- `feature/memory-audit`: no. Its branch ref is still at `bfadca7b2c` and does not contain the new `readMemoryPluginEntry` UI gating or `createMemoryAuditCronReconciler` cron retry fix.

Also, the separate memory-audit worktree is currently detached, not checked out on `feature/memory-audit`, so don’t treat that worktree state as meaning the branch has the fix.

[user]
attach that branch to the independent worktree again and make sure that branch has the latest update

[assistant]
I’ll re-check the detached worktree and both branch tips so the handoff plan doesn’t accidentally discard detached commits or user edits. Since this turn is in Plan Mode, I can inspect but not run the branch checkout/cherry-pick yet.

The detached worktree is clean. Its detached HEAD is `5acd28e9a8`, one commit ahead of the combined branch fix commit, while the actual `feature/memory-audit` branch ref is still back at `bfadca7b2c`. I’m comparing those tips before choosing the safest branch-reattach command.

There is one important ambiguity before I can make a safe execution plan: the detached worktree has a newer detached commit `5acd28e9a8`, but that commit is on top of the combined branch and appears to overwrite part of the cron retry implementation. I need to know whether to preserve that detached commit or make `feature/memory-audit` match the known-good combined-branch fix.

I’m using the executing-plans skill to apply the agreed branch/worktree plan. I’ll first verify the memory-audit worktree is still clean, then reattach `feature/memory-audit` to the known-good fix commit and run the targeted checks.

The worktree is clean and detached, so I’m moving the `feature/memory-audit` branch ref to `0ab394ea16` and attaching that worktree to it now.

Git reports the detached worktree is in a rebase state even though status looked clean. I’m inspecting the rebase metadata before clearing it, so I don’t discard any uncommitted work.

The rebase is paused while replaying older memory-audit commits onto the fixed combined branch. Since the agreed target is to make `feature/memory-audit` match `0ab394ea16` exactly, I’m aborting that in-progress rebase and then resetting the branch to the known-good fix commit.

The worktree is now attached to `feature/memory-audit` at the known-good commit. I’m confirming the expected symbols are present and then running the three targeted tests from that independent worktree.

All targeted checks passed in the independent worktree. I’m doing a final worktree listing and status check to make sure both branches are attached where expected.

Done.

`/home/syu/openclaw-memory-audit` is now attached to `feature/memory-audit`, and both branches point at the same known-good commit:

```text
feature/combined-flow-qmd-memory-audit-20260527 0ab394ea16
feature/memory-audit                            0ab394ea16
```

Confirmed worktrees:

```text
/home/syu/openclaw              0ab394ea16 [feature/combined-flow-qmd-memory-audit-20260527]
/home/syu/openclaw-memory-audit 0ab394ea16 [feature/memory-audit]
```

Verification passed in `/home/syu/openclaw-memory-audit`:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit-cron.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts`

I did not push either branch.

[user]
I now want to make a PR using this branch feature/memory-audit
what do u think, cause I think it is a better alternative than the current dreaming feature

[assistant]
I’ll ground this in the actual branch state and the feature surface before giving a recommendation. I’m going to compare `feature/memory-audit` against its base, check whether it is PR-ready, and look at how it overlaps with the existing Dreaming path.

The branch is technically PR-able, but it is not a clean “Memory Audit only” branch right now. It points to the same combined commit as `feature/combined-flow-qmd-memory-audit-20260527`, so I’m checking the ahead commits to separate Memory Audit from the extra flow/QMD changes before recommending a PR shape.

My read: Memory Audit is a stronger product direction for controlled memory quality because it stages human-reviewable changes instead of automatically promoting/generated memory. The PR risk is scope, not the idea: this branch currently includes Memory Audit plus task-flow migration fixes and QMD mcporter fixes, so reviewers may see it as a combined PR rather than a focused alternative to Dreaming.

Yes. Right now `feature/memory-audit` has other fixes besides Memory Audit, because we attached it to the same tip as `feature/combined-flow-qmd-memory-audit-20260527`.

Its ahead commits include Memory Audit, but also:

- `5f9f3af57e` `fix: rebuild legacy flow_runs tables`
- `fd2b200455` `refactor: simplify flow_runs rebuild controller logic`
- `ba67a616d4` `test(task-flow): cover hybrid flow_runs migration`
- `fe44a8f52c` `test: avoid task flow registry merge conflict`
- `a5a071b418` `fix(memory): isolate qmd mcporter per agent`
- `508761273b` `fix(memory): preserve mcporter server config`
- `24a80a653e` `fix(memory): keep configured qmd mcporter warm`

So I would not open `feature/memory-audit` as-is if the PR is meant to be “Memory Audit as a better alternative to Dreaming.” It will look like a combined PR with task-flow and QMD mcporter work mixed in.

My recommendation: create a clean PR branch for Memory Audit only, then cherry-pick/apply the latest Memory Audit fix (`0ab394ea16`) onto that focused branch.

[user]
eh, why attach it to the same tip as the combined branch, this branch should contain strictly only the memory audit

[assistant]
You’re right. I attached it to the combined tip because the immediate prior plan explicitly chose the “known-good fix” commit `0ab394ea16`, and that commit was the combined branch tip. That made the worktree correct for the latest fix, but wrong for the branch’s intended scope. I’m going to inspect the old memory-audit tip and the Memory Audit commits so we can plan the cleanup without carrying QMD/task-flow commits.

You’re right to push back. The correct target is not “known-good combined tip”; it is “memory-audit-only plus the two later Memory Audit fixes.” I’m checking whether those later fixes can be reapplied cleanly on the old memory-only tip, so the branch plan is precise.

You’re right. I attached `feature/memory-audit` to the combined tip because I optimized for “latest known-good fix” instead of preserving the branch’s intended scope. That was the wrong scope choice for a PR branch named `feature/memory-audit`.

I’m implementing the cleanup plan now: restore `feature/memory-audit` to the original memory-audit-only tip, then cherry-pick only the two Memory Audit follow-up fixes. I’ll keep the combined branch untouched.

The memory-audit worktree is clean and on `feature/memory-audit`. I’m resetting it to `bfadca7b2c`, then applying only `194e0cdec1` and `0ab394ea16`.

The first cherry-pick hit one expected conflict in `src/gateway/server-methods.ts`: the old memory-audit branch has the handlers, but not registered in the method table. I’m resolving that file narrowly, then I’ll continue the cherry-pick.

This cherry-pick is not a clean Memory Audit patch on the old base: `194e0cdec1` also reflects later gateway lazy-handler architecture. To keep the PR branch strictly scoped and mergeable, I’m switching to the cleaner route: rebuild `feature/memory-audit` from current `upstream/main` and cherry-pick only the Memory Audit commits, not the flow/QMD commits.

The branch is now based on current `upstream/main`. I’m applying the Memory Audit commits in order and will stop to resolve only conflicts that are directly in the Memory Audit surface.

The clean cherry-pick series completed without conflicts. I’m verifying the branch now: first scope check to make sure no QMD/task-flow files are present, then targeted tests.

The branch is now cleanly based on `upstream/main`, ahead by seven Memory Audit commits only, with targeted tests passing. I’m doing final status and branch-pointer checks.

Fixed. You were right: `feature/memory-audit` is now strictly Memory Audit scoped again.

What I changed in `/home/syu/openclaw-memory-audit`:
- Reset `feature/memory-audit` onto current `upstream/main`.
- Cherry-picked only the Memory Audit commits:
  - `feat(memory): add memory audit review queue`
  - `fix(gateway): return memory audit error shapes`
  - `feat: add memory audit dashboard tab`
  - `feat: add memory audit settings controls`
  - `fix(memory-audit): wire gateway review queue support`
  - `fix(gateway): register memory audit handlers`
  - `fix(memory-audit): cron retry reconciliation + UI safe-restart gating`

Current pointers:
```text
feature/memory-audit                            0979c76489
feature/combined-flow-qmd-memory-audit-20260527 0ab394ea16
```

Scope check:
- `feature/memory-audit` is `ahead 7` from `upstream/main`.
- No `src/tasks/*` changes.
- No `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager*` changes.

Verification passed:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit-cron.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/memory-audit.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts`
- `pnpm ui:i18n:sync`
- `pnpm ui:i18n:check`
- `git diff --check`

I did not push the branch.

[user]
make a PR draft from that memory audit branch

[assistant]
I’ll verify the branch and PR prerequisites first, then I’ll give you an execution-ready plan. In Plan Mode I can inspect state, but I won’t push or create the GitHub draft PR yet.

The branch is clean and scoped, but it is currently `ahead 7, behind 40` relative to the local `upstream/main` ref. For a draft PR I’d plan to rebase/update it first, then push to your fork and create a draft against `openclaw/openclaw:main` with a real body.

I’ll include the transcript if the local helper finds a safe high-confidence session. I’m checking that now so the PR-creation plan can specify exactly what body file flow to use.

The rebase completed cleanly. I’m running the focused validation again on the rebased branch, plus a scope check so the PR doesn’t include the QMD/task-flow changes.

Validation passed on the rebased branch. I’ll build the PR body now, append the sanitized transcript if the helper allows it, then push to your fork and create the draft PR.

100 read, 18 write, 276 execute, 4 other; raw tool outputs dropped: 397
````

target.author: syu8384

commands.reason: PR is not opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
